Patch Set 7:

Li,

If you're looking at any of my old patches (I'm excited that you are :)), can you add my new Whamcloud account so I can track it?

Patch Set 7:

It would be good to get an updated % improvement here.  Your note in LU-11775 looks like it's not worth much in your benchmark - 17.0 GiB/s vs 17.4 GiB/s...

But your benchmark is file per process.  Can you try a single shared file benchmark?  This patch improves efficiency by batching, but the main benefit I'd expect is on contention, and the main lock here is per file.

Patch Set 7:

FWIW, I'm a little concerned about taking in such a complex piece of code for what is currently a ~2% improvement in the FPP case.  This codes in Lustre a version of some core kernel stuff...  (That's basically why I didn't pursue this patch - Decent benefit in the SSF case, but it's a bit nasty.)

Curious for updated numbers.  (And for what Andreas thinks, if anything)

Patch Set 7: Code-Review-1

I can't abandon this since it's my old account, but Dongyang and I discussed in Vancouver and we agree it should be abandoned.  Not worth the trouble.
Abandoned

Restored

This may be useful after all...
Uploaded patch set 8.

Patch Set 8:

Ihara,

Andreas re-opened the old gerrit, so let's go back to this one.

Patch Set 8:

Looks good to me.

Patch Set 8:

Two things.

1. I still haven't updated this to support newer kernels, because there's a rename and new API for all the radix tree stuff - It's the same data structure but it's called an XArray now.  So this still won't build with RHEL8.  I'll deal with it later, because...

2. Ihara, I'd like it if you could test this version as well.

I looked again at this, and I was much too conservative in my original patch.  With a little effort, I was able to move almost everything out from under the spinlock, and I'm pretty confident it's safe.  So this should be faster - I'm not sure how much, but I would love to know. ;)  It might be significant, might be trivial...

Patch Set 8:

Code-wise looks good to me.

Patch Set 8:

BTW, if it's easy, it would be nice to get an updated flame graph w/this patch & version.

Patch Set 8: Verified-1

Build Failed 

https://build.whamcloud.com/job/lustre-reviews/65791/ : FAILURE

https://build.whamcloud.com/job/lustre-reviews-patchless/6051/ : SUCCESS

Patch Set 8:

Patrick, see LU-12429, I've tested latest this patch, but didn't help very much either.

Patch Set 8:

So, I can refresh this and add in a macro so it builds on newer kernels.

This does improve write performance in the "single node is busy" SSF case by 25% - which is pretty good.

The downside is the amount of kernel code it pulls in to Lustre.

Does anyone have a strong objection to it?  I don't think it's too much to pull in, in this limited case.

(BTW, I am going to go back to the patch set 7 version.  In patch set 8, I was more aggressive about doing less work under the lock, using some Lustre specific page/mapping properties to allow that.  But Ihara showed that 8 gives no significant benefit and 7 more tightly matches the kernel code.)

parents.subject: LU-10467 target: remove lwi arg from target_bulk_io

commit.subject: LU-9920 vvp: dirty pages with pagevec
commit.message:
LU-9920 vvp: dirty pages with pagevec

When doing i/o from multiple writers to a single file, the
per-file page cache lock (the mapping lock) becomes a
bottleneck.

Most current uses are single page at a time.  This converts
one prominent use, marking page as dirty, to use a pagevec.

When many threads are writing to one file, this improves
write performance by around 25%.

This requires implementing our own version of the
set_page_dirty-->__set_page_dirty_nobuffers functions.

This was modeled on upstream tip of tree:
v5.2-rc4-224-ge01e060fe0 (7/13/2019)

The relevant code is unchanged since Linux 4.17, and has
changed only minimally since before Linux 2.6.

Signed-off-by: Patrick Farrell <pfarrell@whamcloud.com>
Change-Id: Ifff9cd01f8b4e960bb4ebea560b9a9a01376698d
Reviewed-on: https://review.whamcloud.com/28711
Tested-by: jenkins <devops@whamcloud.com>
Reviewed-by: Andreas Dilger <adilger@whamcloud.com>
Reviewed-by: Shaun Tancheff <stancheff@cray.com>
Reviewed-by: Li Dongyang <dongyangli@ddn.com>
Tested-by: Maloo <maloo@whamcloud.com>
Reviewed-by: Oleg Drokin <green@whamcloud.com>
